summary: Added a new decompilation source file for dolphin/dvd/dvderror.c, matching __DVDStoreErrorCode at 87.77% for a 344-byte file. The implementation introduces the DVD error-code lookup table, a helper to map raw error codes to numeric indices, and SRAM storage logic using __OSLockSramEx/__OSUnlockSramEx.
what_changed: Created src/dolphin/dvd/dvderror.c as a new source file.
Added ErrorTable, a u32 lookup table of DVD error values used by the dvderror code path.
Added static helper ErrorCode2Num(u32 errorCode), which linearly searches ErrorTable and returns the matching index or 29 when no match is found.
Implemented __DVDStoreErrorCode(u32 errCode), including two special sentinel error codes 0x1234567 and 0x1234568 mapped to -1 and -2 respectively.

Implemented the normal error-code packing path by taking the upper byte of errCode, looking up the low 24-bit value, clamping upperByte to 6 when errCode >> 24 is at least 6, and adding upperByte * 30 to the stored code.
Stored the computed u8 code into SRAM byte offset 36 via __OSLockSramEx and committed it with __OSUnlockSramEx(1).
smart_moves: Used ARRAY_SIZE(ErrorTable) in the table scan instead of hard-coding the loop bound, which helps preserve correctness if the table size is adjusted during future matching work.
Separated the low-24-bit error lookup into a static helper, keeping __DVDStoreErrorCode close to the observed control-flow structure.
Used explicit SRAM locking and unlocking around the direct byte write, matching the expected OS SRAM access pattern.
Preserved the unusual sentinel handling for 0x1234567 and 0x1234568, assigning them through u8 storage as -1 and -2 rather than replacing them with more abstract constants not evidenced by the PR.
decomp_lessons: For small Dolphin SDK files, matching progress may come from reconstructing compact utility functions plus exact data tables rather than broad architectural changes.
DVD error handling appears to encode both a low-24-bit error-table index and a clamped high-byte class into a single stored SRAM byte.
When matching code that writes SRAM, use the OS-provided lock/unlock functions and preserve raw offsets when no struct field evidence is present.
Do not over-abstract magic DVD error values or SRAM offsets without header or symbol evidence; this PR kept the implementation literal.
naming_conventions: The exported function uses Dolphin-style double-underscore internal naming: __DVDStoreErrorCode.
The helper is named ErrorCode2Num, matching the apparent SDK-style mixed-case naming rather than project-local snake_case.
Local variables use concise camelCase-style names visible in the diff: errCode, errorCode, storedCode, sramPtr, upperByte.
The lookup table is named ErrorTable with an initial capital, consistent with the helper's SDK-like style.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: Declared ErrorTable as u32 values matching the DVD error constants needed by the lookup routine.
Used a size_t loop index in ErrorCode2Num, which may matter for generated code shape on this target.
Masked errCode with 0xffffff before table lookup, preserving the separation between the high-byte category and low-24-bit error value.
Implemented high-byte clamping with a comparison against errCode >> 24 >= 6 and assignment upperByte = 6.
Wrote through a u8 pointer to SRAM and targeted offset 36 directly, likely matching the observed byte store.
review_feedback: No issue comments, review comments, or reviews were present in the provided PR context.
No reviewer rationale was available beyond the merge and the generated match report.
follow_up_queries: Find later PRs modifying src/dolphin/dvd/dvderror.c to see how the remaining 12.23% mismatch was resolved.
Search for __DVDStoreErrorCode declarations or calls to verify header placement and linkage conventions.
Search for SRAM offset 36 or OSRtc SRAM layout definitions to determine whether the raw offset was later replaced by a struct field.
Compare ErrorTable against Dolphin SDK documentation or later repository state for possible table length or initializer corrections.
